reasoning: Both decrypt() and decryptKeyInfo() are directly exported from the package (module.exports in xmlenc.js line 333-338, re-exported via index.js). No non-default options are required to reach the vulnerable code path — the RetrievalMethod branch at line 243 is taken automatically whenever the inline EncryptedKey child is absent from KeyInfo, which is a normal XML-DSIG/XML-Enc structure. There is zero input validation before the sink: the URI attribute value from keyRetrievalMethod.getAttribute('URI') is passed only through .substring(1) (which strips the leading '#' but does not neutralize single quotes or other XPath metacharacters) before being concatenated directly into XPath string literals on lines 246 and 273. The xpath library supports safe $var parameterized bindings but they are not used. The decrypt() entry point accepts raw XML from the caller with no schema validation, no allowlist, and no type checking on any XML attribute values. In SAML, WS-Federation, and similar flows the XML payload is fully attacker-controlled, making the vulnerable parameter user-controlled in typical real-world deployments.
